text:
There's a very good reason why theBBCare unable to broadcast every single event from theParis Olympic Games. The Beeb's coverage has been significantly reduced from previous levels, both on television and online.

Social media users expressed their annoyance that the semi-finals of the men's 100m breaststroke, featuring champion Adam Peaty from 2016 and 2020, was not broadcast live. While the corporation also faced a difficult decision on Sunday as Dan Evans and Andy Murray triumphed at Roland-Garros.

The BBC's Olympic content has been restricted in recent years due to a TV deal struck by Warner Brothers Discovery (WBD) in 2015. The American company purchased the European TV rights for the Games for €1.3billion (£1.09bn), granting it comprehensive pick-and-choose live coverage of every sport across its channels, including TNT Sports, Eurosport and the Discovery+ app.

The fact that the BBC retains any rights at all is due to UK law, reportsthe Mirror. As the Olympics is considered a protected event, a national broadcaster is permitted to broadcast the Games on one linear channel, such as BBC One, and one digital channel, like iPlayer.

Their ability to broadcast the Olympics has taken a hit due to rights restrictions, with significant moments possibly being missed. They explained their position on not securing complete rights for the Games on their website.

In a statement uploaded online, they said: "Quite simply because we take decisions over our spending very seriously as it is licence fee payers' money that we are using. As much as we'd like to, we can't buy everything we want."

For comprehensive coverage of the action, all 4,000 hours of it, enthusiasts must turn to the Discovery+ app. For those yet to join, Discovery+ is enticing Olympic fans with a deal until August 11, offering sign-ups at only £3.99 a month.

Discovery+is the new home of the Olympics. The streamer is set to broadcast 3,800 hours of sport live from Paris.

Sports fans can getDiscovery+ for £3.99a month until the end of 2024 via Amazon Prime's video channels. This also comes with anAmazon Prime 30-day free trial.



Meanwhile, Sky TV subscribers already enjoying Eurosport 1 and 2 can get a Discovery+ Standard Plan at no additional charge. In more positive news for the BBC, there was a huge boost in viewership for the opening ceremony in comparison with the last two competitions.

Despite the poor weather, the ceremony in Paris boasted more BBC viewers than the past two Olympics combined. A whopping 6.8m tuned in to watch Canadian icon Celine Dion serenade the audience with Edith Piaf's classic 'L'Hymne A L'Amour' from the Eiffel Tower, captivating audiences from 5.45pm to 10.40pm on BBC One.

We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

The peak viewing figure hit an impressive 7.9m, marking a high point in engagement since London 2012's grand opening, which attracted a staggering 22.4 million viewers. They were also aided by the minimal time difference as France stands just one hour ahead of the UK, compared to Brazil and Japan.

In Rio de Janeiro, the ceremony started at midnight in the UK and attracted an average of 3.8 million viewers. During Tokyo 2020, the BBC reportedly saw a peak live audience of 2.3 million, which was held midday UK time, with no spectators in the Japanese stadiums due to Covid restrictions.

title: Why aren’t the BBC showing every event live at Paris Olympics? | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Mark Whiley, Patrick Austen-Hardy
description: The BBC are unable to show every event live at the Paris Olympics unlike previous years, and there's a very good reason for it.
